topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The chainsaw’s whine still echoed in my ears, a grating soundtrack to the devastation. I stood at the edge of the clearing, my fists clenched so tightly my knuckles were white against my skin. Where the sapling, my tiny, hopeful offering from a summer afternoon years ago, had stood, there was now only a raw, jagged stump and splintered wood scattered across the damp earth. My breath hitched, a ragged, shallow sound in my chest. I walked toward the remnants, my boots crunching over the debris, each step deliberate, heavy with a simmering heat that radiated off me. "You… you did this?" My voice was low, a dangerous rumble that seemed too large for my throat. I kicked at a piece of bark, the sound sharp and resentful. The injustice of it all coiled tight in my gut, making my stomach churn with a sour, unpleasant feeling.

[story 2]

I stared at the exposed roots, twisted and broken like old bones, and a sudden, intense pressure built behind my eyes. It wasn't just sadness; it was a furious tightening in my chest, a need to lash out. I turned slowly, surveying the cleared space where the small shade tree used to anchor itself. Every fiber of my being vibrated with a sharp, unwelcome energy. I didn't speak immediately. Instead, I walked a tight circle around the wreckage, my jaw locked so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I let out a sharp, frustrated exhale, the air tasting metallic in my mouth. "Unbelievable," I muttered, the word laced with a brittle edge. My hands balled into fists at my sides, the tension in my arms making them tremble slightly. I wanted to tear something down, to make a scene, to somehow force a reckoning from this senseless removal.

[story 3]

The sight of the cleared ground hit me like a physical blow. My entire frame stiffened, every muscle locking into rigid tension. I didn't move for a long moment, just standing there, absorbing the brutal finality of the event. A deep, internal churning began, a hot, restless energy that made my skin prickle. When I finally moved, it was with a swift, jerky motion. I strode toward the stump, my gaze fixed on the severed base. My voice, when it finally emerged, was tight, strained, pitched too high. "How could you just… obliterate it?" I spat the words out, the sound sharp and accusatory. I watched my own hands shake slightly as I gripped a fallen branch, the sheer frustration manifesting as a restless need to destroy something nearby.
